skill.name: module-scaffolder
skill.description: Scaffolds DevPrep AI feature modules with TypeScript templates, path alias guidance, and module quality checks.
skill.summary: Create and validate DevPrep AI feature modules that follow the project architecture.
skill.author: ArieGoldkin

security_audit.summary: Most static findings are false positives from markdown code fences, TypeScript import examples, and ordinary identifiers. The audit confirmed a real path traversal risk in helper scripts that use an unvalidated module name to build filesystem paths, so publication should wait for input validation fixes.

high_findings.title: Path Traversal in Module Name Arguments
high_findings.description: add-component.sh and validate-module.sh assign the module name directly from user input and append it to MODULES_DIR without the lowercase-hyphen validation used by create-module.sh. A crafted module name containing traversal segments could make the scripts inspect or write outside the intended modules directory.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The vulnerable scripts build paths from unsanitized positional arguments, while create-module.sh shows the expected validation pattern. The impact is local filesystem access through Bash scripts, not remote execution or exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: Local Bash Scripts Create and Inspect Project Files
medium_findings.description: The skill intentionally documents and ships Bash scripts that create module directories, generate files from templates, and recursively inspect TypeScript files. This is expected scaffolding behavior, but it requires users to trust community shell scripts before execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The scripts are clearly tied to the advertised scaffolding workflow and do not show network or credential access. The confidence is high that the behavior is legitimate but still security-relevant.

low_findings.title: Documentation Command Detections Are Mostly False Positives
low_findings.description: Many external command detections occur inside markdown code fences and inline examples that explain how to run the scaffolder or describe project structure. These passages are documentation, not hidden execution logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged locations are visible markdown examples and TypeScript import snippets. No evidence found that these markdown passages execute automatically.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto, Browser Storage, SAM, and Network Hits Lack Malicious Context
low_findings.description: The reviewed locations are benign naming, architecture, or placeholder examples, including an API_BASE_URL example and a SYSTEM_DESIGN enum value. No evidence found of credential access, weak cryptographic operations, browser storage scraping, or outbound network behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted review found documentation and placeholder template text rather than executable crypto, credential, or network code. The remaining risk is low because these are scanner keyword matches.

dangerous_patterns.title: Unsanitized Path Construction from Script Arguments
dangerous_patterns.description: Shell scripts construct MODULE_PATH by joining MODULES_DIR with a caller-supplied module name. The missing validation in two scripts makes traversal possible before file inspection or writes happen.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code path is direct and user controlled. The risk is constrained to local script execution but should be fixed before marketplace publication.

dangerous_patterns.title: Community Bash Execution Surface
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill depends on Bash helpers that run sed, find, grep, and wc against project files. These commands support the advertised workflow, but they increase review requirements for a marketplace skill.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are normal local tooling commands with quoted paths and no network calls. They remain dangerous patterns because they execute in the user workspace.

content.value_statement: Feature modules can drift from project architecture when teams create them by hand. This skill generates module structure, starter TypeScript files, and validation guidance for DevPrep AI.
actual_capabilities: Creates DevPrep AI feature module folders for components, hooks, utilities, and types.
Generates starter TypeScript and React files from included templates.
Adds new module components and updates component barrel exports.
Validates module structure, file size, interface naming, any usage, and import patterns.
Documents path alias rules, naming conventions, and the six-folder project architecture.
limitations: Designed for the DevPrep AI frontend layout and may not fit other repositories.
Requires local Bash execution and filesystem write permissions.
Does not generate routes, API endpoints, database models, or tests.
The current helper scripts need stronger module-name validation before publication.

use_cases.title: Create a New Feature Area
use_cases.description: Generate a module skeleton for a new product domain without manually recreating folders and starter files.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team Module Layouts
use_cases.description: Keep component, hook, utility, and type placement aligned with the DevPrep AI architecture.

use_cases.title: Review Module Quality Before Merge
use_cases.description: Run structural checks that flag oversized files, loose types, naming issues, and import pattern drift.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Module
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the module-scaffolder skill to create a new analytics module for DevPrep AI. Show me the files it will create.

prompt_templates.title: Add a Component
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the module-scaffolder skill to add an AnalyticsSummary component to the analytics module and update exports.

prompt_templates.title: Validate a Module
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the module-scaffolder skill to validate the analytics module and explain any structure or TypeScript issues found.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Full Feature Module
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the module-scaffolder references to plan a notifications module with components, hooks, utilities, types, and path alias decisions.

output_examples.output: The skill creates an analytics module with component, hook, utility, and type locations, plus starter files that follow DevPrep AI conventions.

output: Reports whether required folders and barrel exports exist.
Flags files over the line limit, interfaces without the I prefix, any types, and excessive relative imports.

output_examples.output: The skill creates a React component file and adds a matching export in the module component index.

best_practices: Review generated files before adding business logic or committing changes.
Use lowercase module names and PascalCase component names.
Run validation after scaffolding and after adding significant module code.
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill for repositories that do not follow the DevPrep AI folder layout.
Do not pass untrusted or traversal-style module names to the helper scripts.
Do not treat generated placeholder logic as finished production behavior.

faq.question: What does this skill generate?
faq.answer: It generates module folders, starter React components, barrel exports, hooks and utility placeholders, and module type files.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Does it work outside DevPrep AI?
faq.answer: It is tailored to the DevPrep AI frontend path structure and TypeScript conventions.

faq.question: Does it make network requests?
faq.answer: No evidence found of runtime network requests in the reviewed skill files.

faq.question: Why is the security risk high?
faq.answer: Two scripts build paths from an unvalidated module name, which can allow local path traversal if misused.

faq.question: What should be fixed before publishing?
faq.answer: Add the same module-name validation to add-component.sh and validate-module.sh that create-module.sh already uses.
